text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Atending Physician: Oncology (Melanoma Program)
Visit Type: Established Patient Office Visit — Treatment Response/Disease Status Review

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Renata Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 57-year-old female with Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma, now presenting for follow-up review of recent imaging and liver biopsy results. Today's visit focuses on discussion of the biopsy-confirmed pathologic complete response at the residual right hepatic site and ongoing treatment planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo has had a remarkable and complicated treatment course over the past approximately two years since her initial diagnosis of Stage IV melanoma. To briefly summarize the relevant history:

She was originally diagnosed with BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma arising from a left posterior shoulder primary (4.2 mm Breslow, Clark level V, ulcerated) with synchronous metastases to bilateral axillary lymph nodes, multiple right flank subcutaneous nodules, and four hepatic lesions (largest 3.1 cm) confirmed on staging PET-CT. Comprehensive NGS demonstrated BRAF V600E (VAF 48%), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, PTEN R130Q (VAF 31%), TP53 R248W, and high TMB at 42 mut/Mb; PD-L1 TPS was 45% by IHC.

Given the rapid clinical trajectory and hepatic disease burden with elevated LDH, she was initiated on first-line dabra 150 mg BID plus tram 2 mg daily. She had an early, striking response — the 8-week restaging CT showed an approximately 62% reduction in target lesion burden with near-complete resolution of hepatic and subcutaneous disease. The 6-month restaging similarly showed continued partial response with approximately 68% overall reduction. However, at approximately 8 months, she developed acquired resistance to the BRAFi/MEKi doublet with progressive hepatic disease, new bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy, and new pulmonary nodules, with LDH rising to 512 U/L. Her disease course on targeted therapy was complicated by two episodes of Tafinlar-related pyrexia (the second requiring a brief ED visit and subsequent dose reduction of Tafinlar to 100 mg BID), as well as grade 1 rash and hand-foot syndrome.

Given progression on targeted therapy, she was transitioned to second-line ipi 1 mg/kg plus Opdivo 3 mg/kg Q3W (CheckMate-067 dosing schema). Her course was complicated by grade 3 immune-mediated colitis during cycle 3, requiring hospitalization, high-dose IV methylprednisolone transitioning to oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day with slow taper, and permanent discontinuation of ipi. She recovered well with the steroid taper; flexible sigmoidoscopy confirmed colitis resolution prior to Opdivo rechallenge. Despite the toxicity, her initial imaging at approximately 10 weeks from combination initiation (during the steroid taper) already demonstrated a partial response — hepatic lesions and axillary nodes significantly reduced. She was then rechallenged on nivo monotherapy 480 mg IV Q4W as maintenance.

On nivo maintenance, she developed immune-related hypothyroidism (TSH peaking at 9.2 mIU/L, free T4 0.7 ng/dL), managed with sequential levothyroxine dose uptitrations to 125 mcg daily. Serial restaging has shown progressive deepening of her response: at 6 months of nivo maintenance, hepatic lesions had diminished to 0.6 cm and 0.3 cm with pulmonary nodules no longer visualized; at 12 months of nivo maintenance, she achieved near-complete response with a single residual sub-centimeter right hepatic lesion measuring 0.4 cm, with LDH fully normalized at 162 U/L.

Given the near-complete radiographic response and the residual hepatic lesion's small size and stability, a multidisciplinary decision was made to perform an image-guided core needle biopsy of the residual right hepatic lesion to assess for treatment viability. This biopsy was performed by interventional radiology without significant complication. Results have now returned and are being discussed at today's visit.

Today, Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo reports feeling genuinely well. She has resumed full-time work as a landscape architect. She reports no abdominal pain, no right upper quadrant discomfort, no diarrhea (bowel habits normal at 1-2 formed stools daily, a significant improvement from her colitis nadir), and no dyspnea. She has not noticed any new skin lesions, lumps, or bumps. She is tolerating levothyroxine 125 mcg well with no symptoms of hypo- or hyperthyroidism. She denies fever, chills, night sweats, or unintentional weight changes. She is managing her sun exposure carefully with SPF 50+ sunscreen daily and protective clothing given her ongoing dermatology surveillance.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: No fever, no chills, no night sweats. Weight stable. Energy levels good, no significant fatigue.
- HEENT: No headaches, no visual changes, no diplopia.
- Pulmonary: No cough, no shortness of breath at rest. Mild exertional dyspnea with strenuous activity only, felt to be deconditioned, improved from prior.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity edema (resolved; previously had peripheral edema on dabra).
- GI: Bowel movements 1-2 per day, formed, no blood, no mucus, no cramping. No nausea or vomiting. No anorexia.
- GU: Normal urinary frequency, no hematuria.
- MSK: No arthralgias, no myalgias (mild on prior dabra, now resolved).
- Neurologic: No focal weakness, no sensory changes, no headaches.
- Endocrine: Well-controlled hypothyroidism on levothyroxine; no cold intolerance, no constipation, no hair changes, no puffiness.
- Integumentary: No new skin lesions noted by patient. No rash. Prior rash on bilateral forearms resolved.
- Psychiatric: Mood good, appropriate affect, engaged and upbeat. Some ongoing anxiety around cancer surveillance.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital Signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 68 bpm (regular), RR 14/min, Temp 37.0 degrees C, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight 64.2 kg (BMI 23.1 kg/m2). Height 166 cm.

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished female. Alert and oriented x3. No acute distress. Pleasant and engaged.

Skin: No new suspicious pigmented or non-pigmented lesions on visible skin exam. Healing scar, right posterior shoulder (wide local excision site), well-healed without concerning features. No subcutaneous nodules palpated. No rash. No hand-foot changes.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae non-icteric. No oral lesions.

Lymph Nodes: No palpable cervical, supraclavicular, or infraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Bilateral axillary regions without palpable lymphadenopathy. No inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Chest/Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, wheezes, or rhonchi.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No peripheral edema.

Abdomen: Soft, non-distended, non-tender including in the right upper quadrant. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated on palpation. No appreciable masses. Normal bowel sounds.

Extremities: No peripheral edema bilaterally. Pulses 2+ throughout. No rash on forearms or palms/soles.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. No focal deficits.

Performance Status: ECOG PS 0.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today):
- CBC: WBC 6.1 x10^3/uL (normal differential), Hgb 13.4 g/dL, Plt 234 x10^3/uL
- CMP: Sodium 139 mEq/L, Potassium 4.1 mEq/L, Creatinine 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60), Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, ALT 22 U/L, AST 19 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 68 U/L, Albumin 4.2 g/dL
- LDH: 161 U/L (normal, ULN 250 U/L)
- TSH: 2.6 mIU/L (within normal range on current levothyroxine 125 mcg)
- Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal)
- Morning cortisol: 14.8 mcg/dL (normal; checked for completeness given steroid history)
- Urinalysis: Normal, no protein

---

IMAGING RESULTS (reviewed today, obtained approximately 2 weeks prior):
CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with Contrast (most recent, reviewed at today's visit): Bilateral axillary lymph nodes remain at normal size — right axilla 0.4 cm, left axilla 0.3 cm, essentially unchanged and within normal limits. In the liver, the previously followed residual right lobe lesion measures 0.4 cm and remains unchanged from prior; the second hepatic lesion that was previously tracked has not been identified on two consecutive scans. No new hepatic lesions. No pulmonary nodules. No subcutaneous nodules. No new sites of disease. Overall, the picture is one of near-complete radiographic response, maintained since the last restaging at 12 months of Opdivo maintenance.

MRI Brain (with and without gadolinium, most recent): No evidence of intracranial metastases, no leptomeningeal enhancement, no new lesions. Stable.

Interventional Radiology Biopsy Report (right hepatic lesion, 0.4 cm, image-guided core needle, reviewed today):
The liver core biopsy of the residual right hepatic lesion demonstrates dense fibrotic and necrotic tissue. No viable melanoma cells are identified. Scattered pigment-laden macrophages and hemosiderin deposition are present, consistent with treatment-related regression of prior metastatic disease. Immunohistochemistry: HMB-45 negative, Melan-A negative, S100 focally positive in reactive stromal cells only (not in any atypical cells). The overall pathologic picture is consistent with a complete pathologic response at this site — all that remains is the scar tissue and treatment effect from prior disease. This is extraordinary.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma — pathologic complete response confirmed:
This is wonderful news. The biopsy of the last remaining radiographically detectable site — the residual 0.4 cm right hepatic lesion — has come back showing no viable tumor whatsoever. Just fibrosis, necrosis, and hemosiderin — the fingerprints of disease that was there but is no longer alive. Combined with the near-complete radiographic response on CT (all other previously involved sites — bilateral axillary nodes, pulmonary nodules, subcutaneous nodules — have either disappeared or normalized), this represents a confirmed pathologic complete response in a patient who presented just over two years ago with multifocal Stage IV melanoma including four hepatic metastases and an LDH of 387.

This patient's clinical course reflects the remarkable biology of high-TMB, PD-L1-positive BRAF-mutant melanoma treated with sequential targeted therapy followed by checkpoint blockade. The BRAFi/MEKi doublet provided rapid and clinically meaningful cytoreduction of a highly active disease burden at diagnosis, followed by a durable deep response on anti-PD1 maintenance immunotherapy despite the complication of grade 3 colitis requiring ipi discontinuation. The depth of response to nivo monotherapy is especially notable given that only 3 cycles of ipi/nivo combination were completed before the toxicity forced discontinuation of Yervoy.

Plan:
- Continue Opdivo 480 mg IV Q4W maintenance. She has now completed approximately 15 months of nivo monotherapy (12 months of maintenance following the initial 3-cycle ipi/nivo induction). Per the CheckMate-067 long-term data and emerging evidence supporting planned treatment holidays after confirmed deep/complete response, our current plan is to continue Opdivo for a total of approximately 2 years from the start of combination immunotherapy (ipi/nivo initiation), after which we will discuss an elective planned treatment discontinuation with close surveillance.
- This timeline places the anticipated treatment discontinuation approximately 3 months from today.
- Restaging CT and MRI brain every 12 weeks (to continue through nivo completion and into surveillance).
- LDH, CBC, CMP with each cycle visit.
- TSH every 3 months.

2. Immune-related hypothyroidism (ongoing, stable):
TSH 2.6 mIU/L today — well controlled on levothyroxine 125 mcg daily. Free T4 normal. Continue current levothyroxine dose. TSH to be rechecked in 3 months. Endocrinology is following; this is likely to be permanent given the established pattern of immune checkpoint inhibitor-related thyroiditis with hypothyroid phase. Patient is aware and tolerating well.

3. Adrenal function monitoring:
Morning cortisol normal today. Given the prolonged steroid exposure (high-dose IV methylprednisolone followed by oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day with taper over 6-8 weeks) during the colitis episode, we had been monitoring HPA axis function. Cortisol is now normal and clinically there are no symptoms of adrenal insufficiency. No further routine cortisol testing needed unless symptoms arise.

4. Dermatologic surveillance:
Patient is followed by dermatology for full skin checks every 6 months given (a) prior melanoma, (b) mixed Afro-Caribbean/Eastern European descent with reported significant lifetime sun exposure as a landscape architect, and (c) potential for new primaries. Please continue this surveillance schedule. Patient counseled to continue daily SPF 50+ sunscreen and sun-protective clothing.

5. Colitis history and GI surveillance:
Colitis has been in clinical remission since completion of steroid taper, confirmed on repeat flexible sigmoidoscopy prior to nivo rechallenge. Patient reporting 1-2 formed stools daily, no blood, no mucus. GI remains available for re-evaluation if any GI symptoms recur on continued Opdivo. Patient reminded to notify clinic immediately for any change in bowel habits, increase in stool frequency, or rectal bleeding.

6. Hypothyroidism (underlying, pre-existing):
Patient had pre-existing hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 75 mcg prior to cancer diagnosis; current dose of 125 mcg encompasses both baseline requirement and the immune-related thyroid destruction component. Will continue to monitor.

7. Psychosocial/Survivorship:
Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo has responded to today's extraordinary news with significant emotional relief and gratitude. She has been engaged in our survivorship clinic since achieving partial response. Given the confirmed pathologic complete response, I discussed the realistic but cautious optimism appropriate for this situation: while Stage IV melanoma has no defined "cure," durable complete responses of this nature — particularly with documented pathologic absence of viable tumor — represent a dramatically altered prognosis compared to the natural history of this disease without therapy. I encouraged continued engagement with survivorship services, ongoing physical activity, and emotional support as available.

Follow-up: Return to clinic in 4 weeks for next Opdivo infusion (Cycle continues). Repeat labs at that time including CBC, CMP, LDH, TSH. Next restaging imaging at approximately 12 weeks.

Time spent in counseling and coordination of care: 45 minutes (>50% of encounter).

All questions answered. Patient verbalized understanding of the plan and expressed enthusiasm for the anticipated treatment holiday discussion at the next major restaging visit.

— Dictated by: Attending Oncologist, Melanoma Program
— Electronically signed
